title: What Private Equity Does Differently: Evidence from Life Insurance
abstract: This paper studies how private equity creates value and its consequences for consumer welfare in the insurance industry, where PE investments grew tenfold following the financial crisis. PE firms add value through regulatory and tax arbitrage that increases profits relative to their non-PE counterparts. Crucially, the impact on consumer welfare is nuanced: in the short run, consumers benefit from more favorably priced products. But the arbitrage strategy also exposes them to more risk, as annual expected losses scaled by capital buffers rise by 50 percentage points. This creates the possibility of consumer harm in the event of a downturn.

author.Natasha Sarin: Yale Law School and Yale School of Management,USA

article.1 Growth of PE in Insurance:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Manju Puri Manju Puri Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

For the last four decades, leveraged buyouts have been an important corporate organizational form.Jensen (1986,1989) offered an early prediction that private equity (PE) would outperform public corporate structures, and a long literature has demonstrated that PE does indeed deliver value-enhancing changes to portfolio companies (Kaplan 1989;Kaplan and Schoar 2005;Kaplan and Strömberg 2009;Robinson and Sensoy 2011;Robinson and Sensoy 2013;Harris, Jenkinson, and Kaplan 2014;Robinson and Sensoy 2016).

Since then, the literature has turned to consider how PE adds value and how its value creation affects welfare. We study these questions by examining the life insurance industry, where PE investments grew tenfold following the financial crisis, from $23 billion in 2009 to $250 billion in 2014.

We show that PE firms add value by quickly taking advantage of opportunities for regulatory and tax arbitrage that increase profits relative to their non-PE counterparts. Interestingly, we find that the story with respect to welfare is more nuanced in this setting than in past work, which has typically painted an extreme picture of PE, as either delivering consumer benefit (Davis et al. 2014;Bernstein and Sheen 2016;Fracassi, Previtero, and Sheen 2022) or causing consumer harm (Eaton, Howell, and Yannelis 2020;Gupta et al. 2021). In the insurance industry, gains to consumers are immediate in the form of more favorably priced life insurance products. However, PE-backed insurers’ arbitrage involves holding less capital to back risky portfolio investments. As a result, expected losses for PE-backed firms far outpace losses for non-PE counterparts. This creates the possibility that consumers will see harm in the event of a downturn.

Our paper begins by collecting a novel sample of private equity acquisitions of life insurance companies between 2005 and 2014. This requires hand-collecting data on the ownership structure of 1,021 life insurers, including matching firm names and individuals who work at PE firms to those who appear on organizational charts provided in regulatory filings. Over our sample period, we document 57 PE acquisitions of life insurance subsidiaries, primarily in the fixed annuities business, with PE’s share of sales growing from 3% of sales to 20% of the market.

We first focus on how private equity creates value. We show that in the insurance setting, PE parents take advantage of a postcrisis opportunity for regulatory arbitrage. There are essentially no PE takeovers in life insurance prior to this arbitrage opportunity and an explosion of PE activity following it, asFigure 1shows. This stylized fact motivates our empirical analysis, which finds that PE parents make immediate and substantial changes to their subsidiaries’ bond portfolios, moving out of highly rated corporate bonds and into poorly rated private-label asset-backed securities (ABS), increasing their holdings of private-label ABS by two-thirds of the industry average. Granular regulatory data allows us to clearly pinpoint when these changes occur, and we show that PE parents deliver them within days of ownership changes.

In the past, such shifts would have led to higher capital requirements. However, a postcrisis regulatory change decoupled capital charges for private-label ABS from credit ratings, allowing PE-backed insurers to increase their junk ABS holdings without simultaneous increases in their capital charges.1So, one driver of PE’s value creation is the identification and exploitation of this postcrisis regulatory loophole. This arbitrage appears unique to PE-owned insurers: We find no evidence that non-PE counterparts learn from and later take advantage of this opportunity.

On the other side of the balance sheet, PE parents also appear to take advantage of opportunities for tax arbitrage through reinsurance with foreign affiliates, transactions thatKoijen and Yogo (2016)refer to as “shadow reinsurance.” As much as 60% of shadow reinsurance activity in the insurance industry writ large from 2011 to 2014 is attributable to the insurance subsidiaries of a single private equity firm that faces a 0% corporate tax rate in its foreign domicile.

We test for time-series differences between PE-owned insurers and their non-PE-owned counterparts and find no evidence of differential trends preacquisition. This helps us exclude the possibility that PE firms target insurers already aggressively taking advantage of arbitrage opportunities. Instead, PE firms actively and immediately deliver these changes to their subsidiaries’ portfolios. Matching estimators and high-frequency event studies yield results similar to our baseline difference-in-differences specifications. While PE ownership is not randomly assigned, the fact that multiple approaches paint a similar picture bolsters our causal interpretation.

Next, we will consider the consequences of this value creation for consumers. We find clear evidence of consumer gains from PE ownership: The average annuity returns offered by PE-backed insurers are above the 75th percentile for their non-PE-backed counterparts. But we also observe the potential for consumer harm in the event of a downturn. Specifically, PE-backed insurers achieve higher returns by taking advantage of arbitrage opportunities to add risk without commensurate capital charges: on average, PE-owned insurers’ capital charges across all bond holdings are 20% lower than they would have been absent the crisis-era regulatory change. Annual expected losses scaled by capital held rise by 50 percentage points after PE owners take control. This shift appears to be ignored by rating agencies, making it difficult for consumers to appreciate: many PE-backed insurers are rated A- to B++; ratings commensurate with their risky bond holdings would be several notches lower.

Our results add texture to the literature, which has tended to find that PE delivers one of two extremes: in some settings, PE delivers changes that improve welfare, for example, by improving restaurant and workplace safety (Bernstein and Sheen 2016;Cohn, Nestoriak, and Wardlaw 2021), launching new products and expanding geographically (Fracassi, Previtero, and Sheen 2022;Howell et al. 2022), and bolstering failed banks, so small businesses—and local employment—grow faster (Johnston-Ross, Ma, and Puri 2020).2In others, PE improves profits at the expense of consumers, for example, in higher education by driving up tuition costs and decreasing graduation rates for students (Eaton, Howell, and Yannelis 2020) and in healthcare, by decreasing staffing and increasing mortality for nursing home residents (Gupta et al. 2021). We find evidence of both: consumers benefit from better-priced annuities products but face the prospect of losses in the event of a downturn.

Recent work byEwens, Gupta, and Howell (2022)finds ambiguous welfare consequences associated with PE entry in the newspaper industry, concluding that although newspaper exit declines following PE acquisitions, so too does civic engagement, so “private equity ownership may be poorly aligned with the public good.” Our conclusion is more nuanced: private equity ownership is aligned with consumer welfare in the short run in the form of more attractive insurance products but may not be in the longer run in the event of downturn. An open question is, “who will bear the cost of an adverse event?” Taken together, our results and the existing literature suggest that it is a mistake to view PE along naive lines, as unambiguously beneficial or harmful to consumers.

What is unambiguous is that PE owners respond to regulatory incentives aggressively. We find that PE-owned insurers, but not non-PE-owned ones, immediately exploit regulation providing an arbitrage opportunity; similarly, recent work byGandhi, Song, and Upadrashta (2020)finds that regulation that supports competition is more effective at encouraging quality improvements at PE-owned firms relative to non-PE counterparts. For policy makers, then, sophisticated PE actors can serve as a bellwether. A careful study of their behavior can help us understand both the intended and unintended consequences of policy interventions.
Life insurers’ assets are substantial: Collectively, they represent almost half of the assets of the banking industry, but they have historically received much less academic attention than banks. Between 2009 and 2014, asFigure 1shows, PE investments in the life insurance industry grew tenfold. This rise precisely coincides with a change in regulatory treatment of private-label ABS that we will discuss in more detail in Section 1.1. Introduced in 2009, this arbitrage opportunity seems related to PE’s entry in the insurance industry. This stylized fact motivates our empirical analysis.

Between 2009 and 2014, purchases of life insurance subsidiaries represented about 10% of all PE transactions (by assets). These purchases tended to be in the fixed-annuities business, which grew to represent more than 25% of the life insurance market by the end of this period.3By the end of 2014, PE-owned insurers accounted for 20% of new underwriting in this line of insurance products.4Three PE firms in our sample drive a significant share (around 25%) of the total acquisitions we observe. Given their prominence among a relatively small sample (i.e., 57 total acquisitions), our results can be well understood as a case study for the ways in which PE creates value and its impact on consumer welfare.

PE ownership in insurance is of growing policy import and has attracted regulatory attention. As early as 2014, the New York State Superintendent of Financial Services (New York State DFS 2014) proposed heightened review of PE’s insurance investments out of concern that PE’s short-term incentives may not align with the long-term interests of policyholders. More recently, the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) has responded to this trend and released a list of regulatory considerations that apply for PE-owned insurers (available on the NAIC’s website), including “material increases in privately structured securities.” The International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) has also emphasized “riskier capital, liquidity and investment strategies” associated with PE ownership (IAIS 2021).

Industry observers, too, have honed in on the growth of PE investments in the insurance industry as an important trend, noting the industry’s attractive “value creation playbook” that affords PE the opportunity to “achieve stronger investment returns largely by rotating the asset allocation into classes that are higher risk and higher return” (Balasubramanian et al. 2022).

1.1 New arbitrage opportunities in insurance

PE’s entry in the insurance industry coincides with new opportunities for regulatory arbitrage, which are potential sources of value creation.

1.1.1 Changes in capital treatment of private-label ABS

The capital treatment of insurers’ private-label ABS has changed sharply in recent years. Prior to the financial crisis, capital requirements set by the NAIC depended on the credit ratings of insurers’ bond holdings. Using the Moody’s classification, NAIC-1 included bonds rated Aaa to A2 (prime and high-investment-grade), and NAIC-6 included bonds rated Caa3 and below (“default imminent”).

During the financial crisis, rating agencies dramatically downgraded structured bonds: by July 2009, only 36% of precrisis AAA-rated CDO tranches remained AAA (Coval, Jurek, and Stafford 2009), threatening destabilizing fire sales by insurers (Ellul, Jotikasthira, and Lundblad 2011). To provide some capital relief, beginning with year-end 2009, the NAIC changed the risk-classification methodology for structured securities, assessing capital charges based on the difference between the book values at which the securities were held and estimated fair values. Thus, below-investment-grade ABS can (and often does) have an NAIC-1 rating if held at sufficient discount (reducing the risk weight from as high as 30% to below 1%).

AsHanley and Nikolova (2021)highlight, during the financial crisis, the effect of this change was to encourage insurers to realize losses on their existing portfolios, helping avoid fire sales. But, as they point out, it is a “mystery” why the regulatory change applies to new purchases of ABS. The implications for regulatory capital requirements are substantial.Becker, Opp, and Saidi (2022)show that the design effectively eliminated capital requirements for nonagency MBS, resulting in greater asset risk exposure for insurers.5

We add two new facts to this discussion: First, PE-backed insurers disproportionately take advantage of this regulatory change. Second, for these subsidiaries, the decrease in capital requirements arises from acquisition of poorly rated ABS, rather than a reduced propensity to sell low-rated bonds. These new purchases begin within days of PE acquisition, as we will document below.

1.1.2 Increase in shadow insurance

Reinsurance is essentially insurance for an insurance provider. The basic motives for reinsurance in the life and annuities business are fourfold: risk transfer, underwriting assistance, capital management, and tax management (Koijen and Yogo 2016).

Koijen and Yogo (2016)focus on the rise of what they term “shadow insurance,” where liabilities are ceded to unrated affiliates outside the scope of U.S. insurance regulation. They document a large rise in shadow reinsurance since 2002, which they attribute to insurers’ successful avoidance of heightened capital requirements. Importantly, this opportunity for capital arbitrage did not exist in the annuities business, yet reinsurance in annuities has grown significantly since the crisis, growth that coincides with the advent of PE ownership of annuities providers.

An alternative rationale for shadow insurance is tax management. According toCredit Suisse (2017), some PE owners use reinsurance to achieve effective tax rates as low as 7%, well below the statutory rate in our sample period of 35%. Recently, reinsurance has been ceded primarily to a non-U.S. domicile that currently has a zero corporate tax rate. A PE-backed insurer operating there has agreed to a zero corporate tax rate through 2035, even if the tax treatment changes for other firms. Thus, tax avoidance may be growing motivation for reinsurance, even though reinsurance for the pure purpose of tax management is disallowed in the United States (Koijen and Yogo 2016).
article.2 Data:
2.1 Private equity ownership

Using SNL Financial’s “Insurance Statutory Financial” (ISF) database, we identify life and annuities insurance firms that operated between 2005 and 2014 with a registered NAIC code. We construct an original panel data set of these firms by ownership structure. Our data set designates each firm as either “PE-owned” or “non-PE-owned” in each quarter of existence during this period.

Although SNL identifies firms that have PE investors, this classification system is imprecise: it often fails to correctly identify the transition to PE ownership and does not provide complete data on ownership shares.

We therefore manually collect data on PE ownership using quarterly NAIC LIFE-QS and LIFE-AS regulatory filings. These filings provide often complex organizational charts disclosing ownership stakes of all large investors. (Internet Appendix Figure B.1shows an example.) PE ownership is not always straightforward to detect, as principals at PE firms, rather than the firms themselves, are occasionally listed as owners. In these cases, we supplement regulatory filings by searching for relationships between the individuals listed and PE firms and transaction announcements. For insurers we determine are PE-owned, we use Form A applications seeking approval for acquisitions of control or mergers to determine the precise date of the PE transaction and whether the acquired insurer is merged or renamed. In cases when SNL is missing data, we search through news reports related to the insurers in our sample.

Individual insurance firms are often subsidiaries of multilevel holding companies. We consider an insurance subsidiary to be PE-owned if it is a subsidiary of a holding company with either a single PE investor or a consortium of PE investors owning a majority of the holding company’s stock. We exclude from our sample firms with PE investors that collectively own less than 50% of the company.6

Our panel structure allows us to track individual insurance subsidiaries both pre- and post-PE ownership, with two limitations. First, when an insurance subsidiary is closed or merged into another insurer acquired by the PE acquirer, it is no longer a standalone observation in our sample. Second, when PE-backed insurers purchase a “book of business” from a non-PE-backed insurer—a portfolio of assets and annuities liabilities—we do not capture this transaction as a PE transaction. That is, PE transactions in our sample involve the transfer of an entire insurance subsidiary to a PE acquirer.

We limit our analysis to the 1,021 life and annuities insurance firms that report their assets between 2005 and 2014. We require data on general account bond holdings to exist for the duration of our sample period. We also drop from our analysis six insurers that are PE-owned for our entire sample. After applying these criteria and excluding insurers with minority PE stakes, we are left with 960 insurers, of which 57 are PE-owned for at least one, but not all, quarters, between 2005 and 2014. Our sample period coincides with rapid growth of PE ownership in the life insurance industry. Private equity grew from representing just 0.5% of assets in 2010 to 4% of assets by 2014.

2.2 Data on insurers

We focus primarily on insurers’ general account bonds, which represent on average over two-thirds of their total assets. General account assets support liabilities with guaranteed returns like term life insurance and fixed-rate annuities. Products with investment losses/gains passed through to consumers, like variable annuities and variable life insurance, are backed by assets held in segregated accounts (Berends et al. 2013;Chodorow-Reich, Ghent, and Haddad 2021).

We obtain data on bond holdings (Schedule D) from SNL’s “Insurance Investment Holdings” database. We select only bond holdings with non-negative fair values. This search yields a total of about 973,000 insurer-quarter-bond observations. We also obtain data on transactions (reported quarterly with acquisition and disposal dates). For private-label ABS, we obtain information on bond credit rating histories from Moody’s. Beyond the bond portfolio, we collect data on the extent to which PE-owned insurers increase shares of “alternative investments” like joint ventures and limited partnerships. We also obtain data on expense ratios to assets.

We classify each observation by NAIC risk category (NAIC 1 through NAIC 6) and asset class (corporate bonds, federal government bonds, state and public utilities bonds, foreign government bonds, private-label ABS, federal government ABS, and hybrid securities).7Internet Appendix Table A.1provides definitions of the key variables in our study, andInternet Appendix Table A.2spells out the credit ratings traditionally associated with each NAIC risk category and associated capital charges.

Table 1summarizes the bond portfolio composition of firms in our sample as of year-end 2014. We report summary statistics for the average PE-owned and non-PE-owned firms. Corporate bonds and private-label ABS are the first and second most common bond types for PE-backed insurers, representing on average 48.1% and 22.3% of all bond holdings, respectively. It is striking—and relevant for our subsequent analysis—that PE-owned firms tend to have a much larger share of their assets invested in private-label ABS than non-PE-owned firms (whose share in private-label ABS averages only 7.4%). By regulatory bond risk category, nearly all bonds (over 97% for both PE-backed and non-PE-backed firms) are within the first two NAIC categories.

To test whether PE firms exhibit superior investment skill, we also collect credit ratings and bond characteristics for corporate bonds and ABS from Mergent FISD, and data on transaction dates and prices from the Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine (TRACE) database.

On the liability side, we collect data on shadow insurance and shadow reinsurers. FollowingKoijen and Yogo (2016), we rely on reinsurance agreements for U.S. life insurers from Schedule S filings provided by A.M. Best Company (a ratings agency focused on the insurance industry) for 2005–2014. These statements are filed annually by individual insurance companies. We also acquire ratings information for insurance firms from A.M. Best.

2.3 Annuity rates

To ascertain the impact of PE investments on product pricing, we use data from Annuity Rate Watch (ARW). ARW began providing data on fixed-annuity offerings, primarily for use by insurance brokers, over two decades ago. It maintains comprehensive information on fixed annuities and monitors over 1,400 products from over 80 different carriers, including many in our sample of PE-backed insurers.

We obtain data for a single date (April 2017) and restrict our analysis to insurers with an A.M. Best rating of A- or higher, creating a comparable sample. Because annuity products differ substantially in specific terms, we focus on 5-year multiyear guaranteed rate annuities (MYGAs), a relatively homogeneous product, currently offered by most carriers in the ARW database. MYGAs are fixed-rate annuity contracts that function essentially as CDs, with a penalty for early withdrawal. We gather specific details about the MYGAs in our sample, including their minimum value, “yield-to-surrender,” the presence of market value adjustments, and penalty-free withdrawal limits. Granularity about the annuity offering terms allows us to determine which products are most comparable.
article.3 PE Ownership and Arbitrage Opportuniti:
PE owners’ response to arbitrage opportunities is much sharper than in the insurance industry at large. In the insurance industry, this is how PE adds value for shareholders. As we discuss in Section 1, two key dimensions are of interest: shifts toward risky private-label ABS and reinsurance.

3.1 Shift to private-label ABS

Our baseline empirical approach relies on difference-in-differences regressions that exploit changes in insurers’ ownership structures. We compare insurers that transition from non-PE-owned to PE-owned to insurers that never experience a PE investment.

These regressions use the following structure:

whereiandqdenote the insurer and year-quarter,tidenotes the year-quarter in which insureriis acquired by a PE firm (or infinity if insureriis not acquired), andaiandgqrepresent firm and year-quarter fixed effects respectively.PEi,q(⁠1q≥ti⁠) is therefore a dummy with value one if the firm is PE-owned in a given year-quarter and zero otherwise. We include insurer fixed effects to control for time invariant characteristics, addressing concerns that PE firms may be acquiring firms that follow different asset-allocation strategies ex ante. We include year-quarter fixed effects to control for possible time trends affecting insurers’ portfolios, and double-cluster standard errors by insurer and year-quarter. We winsorize dependent variables for our regressions within type (PE-backed firm vs. non-PE-backed firm) at the 5th and 95th percentiles to ensure that outliers do not drive our results.8

We begin by examining the impact of PE ownership on asset allocation at the asset class level.Table 2displays the point estimates, followingEquation (1), forPEi,qfor each major asset class. We observe that PE-backed insurance subsidiaries substitute away from corporate bonds in favor of private-label ABS holdings. Specifically, they decrease their share of corporate bonds by over 7 percentage points and increase their private-label ABS holdings by over 6 percentage points. This change corresponds to a 16% reduction in these insurers’ share of corporate bonds (relative to the industry average of 46%) and around a 66% increase in the share of the bond portfolio that is private-label ABS (relative to the industry average of 9.5%).9

The coefficient forPEi,q⁠,β, captures shifts above and beyond general industry trends as we control for year-quarter fixed effects.Table 2also includes a dummy for quarters in years 2010–2014 to illustrate these trends.10The life insurance industry as a whole is moving toward corporate bonds and marginally away from private-label ABS during this period.

3.2 Substitution for high-yielding junk bonds

Although these regression results point to a clear change in the bond portfolios of PE-backed insurers, they say nothing aboutwhyPE firms shifted so substantially into private-label ABS holdings, nor do they allow us to assess whether substitution away from corporate bonds in favor of private-label ABS is associated with an increase in risk.

To examine the effect on risk, we first look at whether there were changes in the NAIC classifications of PE-owned-firm bond portfolios. In particular, we ask whether the shift out of corporate bonds led to a reduction in holdings of relatively safe corporate bonds (NAIC-1) and an increase in holdings of relatively risky securities (NAIC 4, 5, or 6). InTable 3, we see that there is no statistically significant difference in bond portfolio composition by NAIC category between PE-owned and non-PE-owned insurers. Panel A ofTable 4shows that, even within private-label ABS, PE-backed insurers do not hold riskier securities. At first glance, PE-backed insurers do not appear to take on additional risk, at least as measured by regulatory risk classifications, relative to non-PE-owned firms.

LikeBecker, Opp, and Saidi (2022)andBecker and Ivashina (2015), we explore whether insurers increase risk within groups of assets that require the same capital charge. InTable 4, panel B, we examine whether PE-backed insurers’ substitution away from corporate bonds in favor of ABS that we document inTable 2is related to this regulatory change. We restrict our sample from Q4 2007 to Q4 2014, as the regulatory change was implemented during the financial crisis. We construct an “alternate NAIC category” for each bond by matching the ABS to Moody’s historical bond ratings. We construct a counterfactual NAIC rating system to see whether PE-backed insurers substitute toward ABS with low credit ratings and no corresponding increase in capital charge because of the NAIC’s new “expected loss” framework.

We see that PE-backed firms take greater advantage of this regulatory change. Although PE-backed and non-PE-backed insurers hold bond portfolios with similar NAIC ratings, panel B ofTable 4shows that PE-backed insurers appear significantly riskier if we focus instead on underlying bond ratings. PE-backed insurers have shifted away from corporate bonds in favor of below-investment-grade private-label ABS bonds. Using the old NAIC rating system (where NAIC ratings were tied to bond credit ratings), the ABS held by PE-owned insurers would have 11.2 percentage points less NAIC-1 bonds (safest) and 7.4 percentage points more NAIC-6 bonds (riskiest bonds, credit rating of D and below). This increase in NAIC-6 bonds is more than 200% of the industry average of 3.3 percentage points.

Following the regulatory change,Table 5shows that both PE-owned and non-PE-owned insurers’ portfolios are affected, as the improved capital treatment lowers the likelihood that insurers sell legacy junk bonds (Becker, Opp, and Saidi 2022). We show that PE-backed insurers go further, increasing their share of junk private-label ABS, as demonstrated by the statistically significant coefficients for ourPEi,qindicator.11

3.3 Time-series evidence supporting causal interpretation

One natural concern is that our results stem from reverse causality: PE firms may identify the insurers in question as attractive investments precisely because they were already most attuned to the implications of regulatory changes. For example, suppose some insurers are always conservative, holding safe, low-yielding assets, while others are more aggressive, actively managing their portfolios in light of developments like changes in capital charges assessed for structured securities. Perhaps PE firms identify aggressive insurers as desirable targets, rather than changing their behavior after taking over.

To separate selection of targets from the treatment effect of PE ownership, inFigure 2, we follow a generalized difference-in-differences approach. Specifically, we estimate

β β ϵ Outcome i , q = ∑ k = − 4 4 ( β k 1 q = t i + k ) + β 5 1 q ≥ t i + 5 + a i + g q + ϵ i , q (2)

for quarterly changes in the share of private-label ABS in insurers’ bond portfolios (panel A), as well as the share of junk bonds within private-label ABS holdings (panel B). The figure plots the coefficientsβk, showing differences in shares in insurers’ portfolios relative to non-PE-owned insurers, along with 95% confidence intervals, in event time.

If aggressive insurers take advantage of the opportunity for regulatory arbitrage and are targeted by PE firms as a result, we should see differences between PE- and non-PE-owned insurers preacquisition. This is not the case: There are no separate trends before buyouts (βkis not statistically different from zero fork< 0), and PE ownership delivers quick changes. Within a quarter of taking over, PE firms increase their subsidiaries’ private-label ABS share (junk bond share within private-label ABS) by an average of 4 (4.5) percentage points per quarter. These are very meaningful changes, at the 98th (92nd) percentile of all quarterly changes for non-PE-owned insurers in our sample. PE-owned firms continue to increase their subsidiaries’ private-label ABS share (junk bond share within private-label ABS) in the quarters following acquisition, with cumulative terminal increase of 11 (14.5) percentage points postacquisition.

We present similar estimates using trading at a weekly frequency. Here, we estimate

β β ϵ Outcome i , w = ∑ k = − 8 8 ( β k 1 w = t i + k ) + β 9 1 w ≥ t i + 9 + a i + g w + ϵ i , w (3)

for weekly shifts in portfolio composition and plot the coefficientsβk.

We see inFigure 3that PE-backed insurers move aggressively into private-label ABS starting in the week of acquisition. This applies specifically for private-label ABS with lower NAIC risk categories in the new system than in the previous system (panel A) and for private-label ABS overall (panel B). Again, we do not see evidence that the insurers targeted by PE owners move into either type of private-label ABS prior to acquisition.

Strikingly, we observe large changes in PE-owned insurers’ investments and risk taking within days of the transition to PE ownership. We do not find systematic changes prior to PE ownership, nor do non-PE-owned insurers make any noticeable adjustments on the same days. This timing leads us to ascribe our results to changes delivered by PE parents.

Insurance regulatory data permits a high-frequency visual event study that allows us to precisely pinpoint the timing of changes in PE-backed insurers’ bond holdings. Net purchases of high yield bonds treated as NAIC-1 (the lowest risk weight) in the new system are of particular interest. We scale bond acquisitions by total insurer assets at the time of acquisition.

For PE-owned firms, almost immediately following acquisition there is a dramatic increase in non-investment-grade bond holdings assessed NAIC-1 capital charges under the new ratings system. On average, purchases of such bonds within the month immediately after acquisition amount to 1% of preacquisition assets.12There is no systematic shift in PE-owned firms’ trading behavior prior to acquisition. Non-PE-owned firms do not make any discernible changes on the same set of days on which PE-owned firms dramatically move into private-label ABS. The bonds purchased by PE-owned firms are rated Ba1 and below yet incur the lowest possible capital charge (risk-based capital charge of 0.4%), an opportunity PE parents in our sample exploit aggressively. The speed at which PE firms shift the composition of insurers’ bond portfolios—a relatively straightforward change to make for those with an understanding of the industry—suggests this reshuffling is part of PE’s acquisition strategy and plan for speedy value creation.

3.4 Results based on matching estimators

Our results in the time series support the interpretation that the involvement of PE parents drives our results rather than sample selection. However, given that PE ownership is not randomly assigned, to further bolster this interpretation we match PE-owned insurers to insurers that do not experience a PE investment but are otherwise similar on observables.

We match on three key dimensions on which insurers may differ that we can observe: business mix, size, and capitalization strategy. As we can only observe insurers’ RBC ratios and liability mix at an annual frequency, we conduct our matching analysis at the insurer-year level. Standard errors are double clustered by insurer and year. We match within insurers that hold private-label ABS in at least one year following the change in their regulatory treatment.

PE-owned insurers are more focused on fixed annuities, tend to be smaller, and are marginally more aggressive in their capitalization strategy than insurers that do not experience a PE investment on average (Internet Appendix Table A.8, panel A). We employ propensity score matching to match PE-owned insurers to non-PE-owned insurers in the year prior to acquisition. Our sample of matched non-PE-owned insurers closely resembles our sample of PE-owned insurers on observables (Internet Appendix Table A.8, panel B).

3.5 No evidence of superior investment skill in PE-backed bond portfolios

PE-backed insurers tilt their bond portfolios away from corporate bonds toward low-rated private-label ABS. It is also possible that PE firms are better able to identify underpriced bonds and hence move away from bonds for which returns only reflect systematic risk. We examine this possibility by directly evaluating subsequent performance for corporate bond portfolios. If PE firms possess superior investment skill, the corporate bonds their subsidiaries retain should outperform the portfolios of non-PE-owned insurers.13

We focus on investment-grade corporate bonds issued between 2009 and 2014, the period in which PE significantly entered insurance and PE-backed insurers moved away from corporate bonds.14FollowingBecker and Ivashina (2015), for each month we sort all newly issued corporate bonds based on the fraction acquired by PE-backed insurers, relative to all life insurers. We divide bonds into two portfolios. Bonds with above-median PE shares enter the “High-PE” portfolio, while bonds with below-median PE shares enter the “Low-PE” portfolio. Bonds remain in their portfolios for a 12-month holding period. We present regressions for which the dependent variable is monthly returns in excess of the risk-free rate, controlling for both risk and liquidity factors. Excess returns for portfoliopin monthmare

We use coupon rates from Mergent FISD and end-of-month prices from transactions reported in TRACE to calculate these returns at the bond level, which are equally weighted to produce portfolio returns.

We find no evidence that PE firms have superior investment skill for corporate bonds (Table 6). The first and fourth specifications use theFama and French (1989)model. The second and fifth specifications and the third and sixth specifications, respectively, control for market returns and liquidity factors. Corporate bonds in the High-PE portfolio had sizeable monthly alphas of about 60-70 basis points. However, corporate bonds in the Low-PE portfolio also had comparable alphas: Corporate bonds were more broadly an attractive asset class during this period. The additional return in the High-PE portfolio, ranging from 3 to 9 basis points, cannot be statistically distinguished from zero.

We also examine PE-backed insurers’ allocations to alternative investments (e.g., joint ventures and limited partnerships). Some industry observers speculate that PE owners offer investment expertise in complex assets. For example,Deutsche Bank (2017)suggests that one PE-owned insurer relies on the PE firm “to achieve an enhanced investment yield by …taking on complexity risk.”

We are not able to directly examine whether PE owners have skill in identifying alternative investments. However, we do observe that PE-backed insurance subsidiaries substitute away from standard asset classes in favor of alternative investments (Internet Appendix Table A.12). Although we are unable to rule out the possibility that PE owners do have skill in alternative investments, we also observe that they make more affiliated investments, typically viewed to be risky and limited in many states.

3.6 Reinsurance and tax arbitrage

To enhance returns, in recent decades, insurance companies have increasingly used “shadow insurance,” or reinsurance to an affiliated (captive) reinsurer not authorized by U.S. insurance regulators.Koijen and Yogo (2016)argue that this rise in reinsurance primarily exploits differences in the treatment of life insurance under GAAP and statutory accounting.

Interestingly, following the financial crisis, shadow insurance contracts have overwhelmingly been initiated by PE-backed insurers. Specifically, between 2011 and 2014, PE-backed insurers accounted for more than 90% of new shadow insurance for fixed annuities and nearly 60% of all new shadow insurance (Figure 5, panel B).

Indeed, less than half of the rise in shadow insurance since 2011 is in life insurance; instead, a significant fraction of recent reinsurance is in the annuities business, where there is no difference between GAAP and statutory accounting rules that is relevant. Much recent reinsurance is in the annuities business (Figure 5, panel A), where there is no opportunity for capital arbitrage, asKoijen and Yogo (2016)discuss. While some portion of the growth in annuities reinsurance may be motivated by potential capital savings, another motivation is tax savings. Much of the growth in shadow insurance in the latter part of our sample is related to reinsurance with a non-U.S. domiciled captive by subsidiaries of one of the PE groups in our sample, likely significantly lowering tax burdens. A subsidiary of one PE group in our sample, domiciled in a jurisdiction that does not impose income or capital gains taxes, has been explicit about the importance of these tax savings and even negotiated tax exemptions through 2035, even if such taxes are applied to other companies.
article.4 Welfare Implications:
Our evidence on how PE operators exploit regulatory and tax arbitrage opportunities demonstrates how PE adds value for shareholders in the insurance industry. We now ask how this value creation affects consumers. Interestingly, our results point to a nuanced story: We see that consumers benefit through better pricing of insurance products, but also face potential losses in the event of a downturn as PE-backed insurers take on more risk.

4.1 Benefits for consumers: Product pricing

PE owners improve profitability through regulatory and tax arbitrage. Here, we ask whether PE-backed insurers pass some of these gains on to consumers in the form of better priced insurance products.

InFigure 6, we compare annuity pricing for subsidiaries of two of the PE groups in our sample to the non-PE-owned insurers in our database. We look at rates on 5-year MYGAs, which function essentially like 5-year CDs with penalties assessed for early withdrawal. Panel A shows pricing for all 5-year MYGAs issued by insurers rated A- or better by A.M. Best. We see that the yield guaranteed by PE-owned insurers is higher than that of other insurers. While there is high variance in yield offerings for different carriers with different terms, the average 5-year MYGA rates for the PE-backed insurers in the ARW sample are above the 75th percentile for the non-PE-backed firms.

One issue in comparing annuities pricing by PE-owned-firms relative to non-PE-owned firms is that it is difficult to find insurance products with identical terms (minimum balances, penalties for early withdrawal, amount that can be withdrawn without penalty, commission terms, etc.). To facilitate comparison, in panel B we focus on high-band or mid-band (with minimum values of over $100,000) MYGAs with market-value adjustments that can transfer some interest-rate risk to consumers. For this subsample, only one PE-backed insurer remains in the ARW sample. It offers a 5-year MYGA at 2.75%, which is higher than the 90th percentile of the remaining non-PE sample.

Aggressive pricing by PE-backed insurers appears to have helped them grow their market share. Indeed, PE-backed insurers’ share of new annuity sales jumped from below 3% in 2009 to nearly 20% in 2017 (LIMRA 2008,2013,2017).

4.2 Potential costs for consumers: Heightened risk

Although PE-backed insurers offer consumers better prices, the regulatory arbitrage strategies that allow them to do so also expose consumers to greater risk. PE-backed insurers hold significantly less capital than they would be required to hold under the previous rating-based treatment of private-label ABS. Ratings used by both retail and institutional customers do not appear to reflect these thinner capital buffers. Following PE entry, expected losses on assets rise relative to capital held, indicating the potential for meaningful consumer losses in a downturn.

Capital requirements for insurers are determined using a composite measure, with different components of risk entering differentRias inEquation (5).

Authorized control level ( ACL ) RBC = R 0 + ∑ i = 1 5 R i 2 . (5)

The asset risk component,R1, is determined as a risk-weighted average based on the share of assets held in different NAIC risk categories. Insurers often quote RBC ratios defined as

RBC Ratio = Total adjusted capital ( TAC ) / ( 2 × ACL RBC ) (6)

in percentage points.15

Table 7shows that for PE-backed firms the asset risk component of capital,R1,overall—not just for structured securities—is 20% lower than it would have been in the previous system. PE-backed insurers are responsible for over 20% of the reduction in risk-based capital documented byBecker, Opp, and Saidi (2022), despite representing less than 5% of the total life insurance assets under management. While required capital levels fall, PE owners could still decide to hold additional capital in reserve. However, this does not appear to be the case: there is no meaningful change in statutory capital levels following PE entry, asInternet Appendix Table A.13shows.

For the largest PE-owned firms in our sample, the reduction in risk-based capital is exceptionally high. In fact, subsidiaries of two of these groups are required to hold 50% less capital overall than they would have been forced to hold had their RBC ratios been calculated with the old NAIC rating system. These firms appear distinct in their knowledge of the insurance industry: their executive teams include those with backgrounds in insurance to help identify and then quickly exploit this opportunity for arbitrage.16

However, this is a broad-based phenomenon: even when excluding these groups, we observe a statistically significant reduction in required risk-based capital inTable 7, albeit of much smaller magnitude. And it is unique to PE-backed insurers: asInternet Appendix Figure A.2shows, we see no commensurate declines in required risk-based capital over time for non-PE-backed insurers. So, it does not appear that non-PE-backed firms learn of and later exploit this opportunity for regulatory arbitrage: the strength of PE’s profit-maximizing incentives is unmatched.

Does less capital translate to lower ratings? Although the primary rating agency, A.M. Best, is not a regulator, its ratings are important to insurers because they are used by both retail and institutional customers.17To investigate what would likely have happened to ratings for PE-backed insurers under the old rating-based system, we use our estimates of the change in the asset risk component,R1, to calculate a lower bound for the impact on RBC ratios.18

Ratings that fully accounted for PE-owned insurers’ private-label ABS holdings could be among the lowest in the A.M. Best universe.Figure 7shows how PE-owned insurers’ actual and adjusted RBC ratios compare with non-PE-owned insurers as of 2014. The regulatory arbitrage pursued by PE-backed insurers exposes consumers to greater risk. We estimate expected future losses scaled by capital buffers to assess this impact. We followCoval, Jurek, and Stafford (2009), who report annualized default rates for investment and speculative-grade bonds based on their ratings. We apply these default rates to insurers’ portfolios to compute their annual expected loss and scale them by regulatory capital held.Table 8(second column) shows that, following PE entry, expected losses scaled by capital held increase by more than 50%.

Consistent with Section 3.5,Table 8also shows that PE owners appear to generate higher returns primarily by taking on more risk. PE-backed insurers do not obtain higher returns than non-PE-backed insurers with similar risk profiles.
article.5 Conclusion:
This paper shows that PE owners in the insurance industry create value for their subsidiaries by pouncing on arbitrage opportunities. Specifically, PE parents make immediate changes to their subsidiaries’ bond portfolios, increasing their holdings of private-label ABS by two-thirds of the industry average without facing commensurate capital charges. Additionally, PE-owned insurers lower their tax liabilities by reinsurance to unregulated foreign subsidiaries.

The existing literature has tended to find that PE operates at one of two extremes: either delivering consumer benefit or causing consumer harm. Unlike other settings, we find evidence of a more nuanced story: in the short run, consumers benefit because they share in PE’s value creation through more favorably priced annuities. But the opportunity for arbitrage created by postcrisis regulatory changes and then exploited by PE owners decreases the proportion of capital insurers hold to buffer against losses, so expected losses scaled by capital rise by 50 percentage points after PE owners take control. This raises the possibility that in the longer run, consumers will be harmed if an adverse event occurs.

Policy makers are increasingly focused on the role of private equity and its consequences for consumer welfare. Our findings suggest painting PE with a broad brush is errant. Further, studying PE activity helps shed light on the consequences—both intended and unintended—of regulatory interventions.
